coredata.dc:title: Smart nanocomposite of carbon quantum dots in double hydrogel (carboxymethyl cellulose/chitosan) for effectively adsorb and remove diquat herbicide: Characterization, thermodynamics, isotherms, kinetics, and optimizing through Box-Behnken Design

coredata.dc:description: This research synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) encapsulated in a chitosan (CS) and carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) matrix. The crosslinking with epichlorohydrin formed (CQDs-CS/CMC) hydrogel beads for effective removal of diquat (DQ) herbicides. Various techniques like XRD, FT-IR, FESEM, EDX, XPS, and nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherm analysis were used to evaluate the textural properties. The textural properties of the CQDs-CS/CMC were investigated through nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms. The surface area was found to be 95.72 m2/g, pore size 6.57 nm, and pore volume 0.313 cc/g. Post-DQ adsorption, these values decreased to 68.44 m2/g, 4.2 nm, and 0.162 cc/g, indicating DQ blocked mesopores and adsorption sites. The study also examined the effects of dosage, pH, temperature, and initial DQ concentration on adsorption, employing equilibrium and kinetic studies. The system conformed to pseudo-second-order kinetics and Langmuir isotherm models. Chemisorption was the main adsorption process, with an energy of 32.3 kJ/mol. Increased metal uptake at higher temperatures shows the process is spontaneous and endothermic. The Box-Behnken Design software identified optimal adsorption parameters: a pH of 8 and a dosage of 0.02 g of CQDs-CS/CMC per 25 mL of DQ solution, achieving 449.6 mg/g adsorption capability. Extensive testing using Design-Expert software substantially improved the adsorption procedure. The assessment of adsorbent stability involved six cycles of adsorption/desorption. Results showed consistent reusability with no significant reduction in removal efficacy. It maintained its initial chemical configuration before and after repurposing, exhibited consistent performance, and reliable XRD results.

head.ce:title: Smart nanocomposite of carbon quantum dots in double hydrogel (carboxymethyl cellulose/chitosan) for effectively adsorb and remove diquat herbicide: Characterization, thermodynamics, isotherms, kinetics, and optimizing through Box-Behnken Design

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This research synthesized carbon quantum dots (CQDs) encapsulated in a chitosan (CS) and carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) matrix. The crosslinking with epichlorohydrin formed (CQDs-CS/CMC) hydrogel beads for effective removal of diquat (DQ) herbicides. Various techniques like XRD, FT-IR, FESEM, EDX, XPS, and nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherm analysis were used to evaluate the textural properties. The textural properties of the CQDs-CS/CMC were investigated through nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherms. The surface area was found to be 95.72 m2/g, pore size 6.57 nm, and pore volume 0.313 cc/g. Post-DQ adsorption, these values decreased to 68.44 m2/g, 4.2 nm, and 0.162 cc/g, indicating DQ blocked mesopores and adsorption sites. The study also examined the effects of dosage, pH, temperature, and initial DQ concentration on adsorption, employing equilibrium and kinetic studies. The system conformed to pseudo-second-order kinetics and Langmuir isotherm models. Chemisorption was the main adsorption process, with an energy of 32.3 kJ/mol. Increased metal uptake at higher temperatures shows the process is spontaneous and endothermic. The Box-Behnken Design software identified optimal adsorption parameters: a pH of 8 and a dosage of 0.02 g of CQDs-CS/CMC per 25 mL of DQ solution, achieving 449.6 mg/g adsorption capability. Extensive testing using Design-Expert software substantially improved the adsorption procedure. The assessment of adsorbent stability involved six cycles of adsorption/desorption. Results showed consistent reusability with no significant reduction in removal efficacy. It maintained its initial chemical configuration before and after repurposing, exhibited consistent performance, and reliable XRD results.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The agricultural industry has experienced substantial growth in output, largely attributed to the application of herbicides for weed management. Within the range of herbicides available, a non-selective contact herbicide has been shown to enhance plant yields; however, its utilization can lead to modifications in soil characteristics and may impact livestock health. An organic bipyridinium herbicide is specifically designed to eliminate undesirable weeds in cereal crops prior to planting or emergence [1]. It is advocated for use in both deep cultivation and shallow tillage practices within agricultural systems. The repeated application of herbicides has the possible to pollute soil and water in areas near root zones, posing risks to ecosystem health due to their toxicity toward zooplankton and crustaceans. These organic pollutants can adhere to sediment and soil surfaces in aquatic environments, often interacting with organic matter and clay particles. Their complex structures can make biodegradation less likely. While various physicochemical treatment methods can potentially eliminate herbicides from contaminated water or soil, these technologies may lead to several negative consequences, including the generation of secondary pollutants, water wastage, and increased energy consumption [2,3].

In contemporary research, the process of adsorption utilizing nanocomposites is increasingly recognized for its efficiency and effectiveness in removing hazardous dyes and toxic heavy metals. This effectiveness is attributed to their significant surface area and reduced particle dimensions. In the context of pesticide pollution management, the encapsulation of zero-dimensional carbon quantum dots has not been thoroughly explored within agricultural soil in the 21st century. Furthermore, there has been a lack of comprehensive investigation into the use of double hydrogels for the removal of herbicides over the past decade [4]. Consequently, given this notable research gap, our study aims to propose the use of carbon quantum dots encapsulated within a double hydrogel matrix as a method for effective herbicide removal from both alkaline soil and alkaline surface water found in paddy fields. The distinctive characteristics and dual network structure of hydrogels may enhance the efficacy of pesticide pollution removal in future applications. Furthermore, the yield response is contingent upon decreasing the initial herbicide levels present in the soil. The adsorption and elimination of herbicides are expected to improve with the integration of these materials. Moreover, the utilization of this nanocomposite could pave the way for novel research avenues and may facilitate the adoption of biodegradable adsorbents within agro-integrated paddy fields, contributing to environmental preservation. A specific design approach is employed to maximize the removal efficiency through the use of an alternative adsorbent [5].

Eliminating of diquat (DQ) herbicides from wastewater is essential given the considerable environmental and health hazards they pose. Diquat exhibits high toxicity toward aquatic life, including fish, amphibians, and plant species, thereby disrupting ecosystems and jeopardizing biodiversity. The substance's chemical stability and lack of susceptibility to natural degradation contribute to prolonged environmental pollution, which may result in accumulation within sediments. This accumulation can have detrimental effects on primary producers such as algae, potentially leading to a series of ecological imbalances [6]. Diquat not only affects aquatic organisms but also compromises water quality by elevating turbidity and causing discoloration, thereby rendering water inappropriate for consumption and recreational activities. Human contact with diquat-contaminated water can lead to immediate health complications, including irritation of the skin and respiratory system, gastrointestinal distress, and, in extreme situations, kidney damage. Additionally, the elimination of diquat contributes to the sustainability of water resources by preventing the contamination of groundwater and soil, thereby safeguarding the availability of clean water for future generations. Given the widespread application of diquat in agricultural practices, its removal significantly alleviates the effects of agricultural runoff, which is a predominant source of water pollution. The thorough removal of diquat is crucial for the protection of ecosystems, the assurance of safe drinking water, and the advancement of environmental sustainability, while also averting secondary contamination and adhering to regulatory mandates [7].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a major research focus in nanomaterials, valued for their photoluminescence and little toxicity, which make them suitable for numerous applications, including biological ones [8]. Their ease of functionalization permits tailored modifications, while resistance to photo-bleaching ensures durability under light exposure, emphasizing their potential in multiple fields. The removal of hazardous heavy metals, harmful dyes, and pesticides from the environment is critically significant. CQDs with their polar practical groups such as COOH and OH, act as effective adsorbents. Hydrogels, particularly double hydrogels with CQDs, effectively remove pesticides. A synergistic effect and improved hydrophilic nature are evident in double hydrogels [9]. Although few studies exist, literature highlights how CQDs enhance pollutant adsorption in hydrogels [10]. CQDs fixed in chitosan (CS) besides carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) present a favorable and sustainable method for the removal of herbicides from wastewater. The notable surface area and the attendance of practical groups on CQDs, in conjunction with the biocompatibility and affinity for pollutants associated with CS, further enhanced by the solubility and stability attributes of CMC, facilitate a synergistic interaction that augments the efficiency of adsorption. This composite material is renewable, biodegradable, and economically viable, showcasing enhanced mechanical strength, selective adsorption, and reusability. Carboxymethyl cellulose's (CMC) hydrophilic nature engages effectively with water-based solutions, while carbon quantum dots (CQDs) enable real-time observation of adsorption [11,12].

The Box-Behnken Design (BBD) offers a systematic method for optimizing adsorption procedures by minimizing the number of experiments and addressing complex relationships among variables like pH, adsorbent quantity, and temperature. Unlike other designs, BBD avoids extreme variable combinations, reducing the risk of impractical or hazardous scenarios [13,14]. The implementation of a comprehensive quadratic model facilitates a precise characterization of nonlinear relationships, thereby allowing for the effective optimization of adsorption efficiency. The incorporation of center points within the Box-Behnken Design (BBD) improves the consistency of estimates concerning experimental variability and serves to affirm the validity of the model [15,16]. This design is specifically tailored for the analysis of multivariable systems, allowing for an in-depth examination of primary effects, interaction effects, and quadratic effects tied to various variables. Such functionality significantly improves the precision and control of the adsorption process. Furthermore, its adaptability to a range of experimental conditions, combined with its economical nature and strong predictive abilities, establishes it as an ideal choice for optimizing complex adsorption mechanisms, particularly in the wastewater treatment industry.
Materials and instruments information concerning to the resources is available in the supplementary section (Tables S1 and S2).

The process of hydrothermal synthesis was employed to produce CQDs. Within this methodology, 3.0 g of citric acid (CA) functioned as the carbon source, paralleling this with an equal mass of urea, also at 3.0 g, which served as the nitrogen precursor in the reaction. The specified constituents were dissolved in 100 mL of deionized bidistilled water. Following this, the combination was transferred into a 150 mL autoclave lined through Teflon made of stainless steel. The autoclave was subsequently subjected to a temperature increase to 180 °C, where it was held constant for a duration of 5 h.

In this process, chitosan (CS) and carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) are combined in a 1:1 weight ratio to achieve the desired end product. This involves the thorough mixing of the two polysaccharide-based polymers in a 50-milliliter solution of 5 % acetic acid in deionized water until complete dissolution of both CS and CMC is accomplished. Following a 30-minute period of ultrasonic behavior applied to the CS/CMC solution, an addition of 2.0 g of CQDs is made, with continuous mixing maintained for a duration of 3 h. After this mixing phase, a 200-milliliter aliquot of a 1.0 M sodium hydroxide (NaOH) solution is incrementally introduced to the mixture in a dropwise manner. Upon contact with the sodium hydroxide (NaOH) solution, the droplets swiftly formed hydrogel beads composed of carbon quantum dots, chitosan, and carboxymethyl cellulose (CQDs-CS/CMC). Subsequently, these hydrogel beads were introduced into an epichlorohydrin solution with the concentration of 0.5 g/100 mL of deionized water, where they underwent crosslinking for a duration of 24 h while being continuously stirred. Centrifugation and freeze-drying were the next steps after the hydrogel beads were created. The beads were then subjected to five rounds of purification using ultrapure water, as explained in Fig. 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Experimental batch studies of DQ via CQDs-CS/CMC hydrogel beads
ce:section.ce:para:
Batch solution experiments aimed at the removal of Diquat (DQ) through the use of CQDs-CS/CMC hydrogel beads were carried out based on a Box-Behnken Design (BBD). In these experiments, 25 mL of an initial solution comprising 400 mg/L of DQ was sited in a 50 mL conical flask, to which varying quantities of CQDs-CS/CMC, reaching from 0.02 to 0.5 g, were incorporated. Subsequently, the solutions underwent rapid mixing in a rotary shaker for a specified duration ranging from 5 to 100 min, operating at a consistent speed of 150 rpm and maintained at ambient temperature (25 °C). To achieve the target pH, adjustments were made using 0.01 M of sodium hydroxide besides hydrochloric acid solutions. Centrifugation was then used to separate the DQ-loaded CQDs-CS/CMC hydrogel beads from the DQ solution. The concentration of DQ was then assessed using a UV–visible spectrophotometer, precisely at a wavelength of 309 nm. The ability of CQDs-CS/CMC hydrogel beads to absorb substances, denoted as uptake capacity (qe), along with the percentage of DQ removal, is determined through the application of the equations presented in Eqs. (1) and (2), respectively.

In this context, qe represents the adsorption capacity of CQDs-CS/CMC hydrogel beads, calculated in (mg/g). The variable R denotes the percentage of herbicide removal achieved. C0 and Ce correspond to the concentrations of DQ at the beginning and end of the adsorption procedure, individually. Moreover, V indicates the volume of the DQ solution utilized, expressed in liters (L), while w signifies the mass of the CQDs-CS/CMC hydrogel beads employed as the adsorbent, measured in grams (g).

Response surface methodology (RSM) represents a systematic approach that employs a range of theoretical and statistical tools aimed at enhancing and optimizing processes. This methodology is effective in reducing the number of experimental trials required while simultaneously providing precise and reliable optimal conditions for the procedure under investigation. In this study, the Box-Behnken Design (BBD), an integral component of RSM, was employed to optimize the removal of DQ using CQDs-CS/CMC hydrogel beads. The independent variables considered in this optimization process included adsorbent dose (A: ranging from 0.02 to 0.05 g per 25 mL), pH levels (B: spanning from 2 to 12), and exposure time (C: varying between 5 and 100 min). The corresponding actual and coded values for these parameters at diverse levels (−1, 0, and +1) are systematically presented in Table S3. The adsorption capacity of DQ (in mg/g) was designated as the response variable linked to the input parameter, and it was represented by a second-order polynomial calculation as demonstrated in Eq. (3):
(3)

In this context, the parameters βo, βi, βii, and βij correspond to the regression factors. The variables Xi and Xj denote the implied independent variables, while Y indicates the response associated with the removal of DQ, and ε signifies the error associated with the model. The analysis of the regression model, including the evaluation of process variables and the computation of the statistical model equation, was conducted with the software Design Expert 13.0 (Stat-Ease, Minneapolis, USA).

Fig. 2(a) shows the X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern that reveals the structural properties of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) as well as those of CQDs-CS/CMC. The analysis of the CQDs, represented by the red curve, shows a broad peak within the 20–30° range, which corresponds to the (002) plane associated with graphitic carbon on JCPDS card no. 41-1487 [17,18]. This observation suggests an amorphous structure since the absence of distinct crystalline peaks further corroborates their disordered arrangement. Conversely, the CQDs that are encapsulated with CS/CMC (represented by the blue curve) preserve a wide peak, indicative of the amorphous characteristics of the CQDs [19]. However, they also display distinct sharp peaks at elevated 2θ values, which point to the emergence of crystalline domains attributable to the structured organization within the CS/CMC matrix. The increased intensity of the CQDs-CS/CMC pattern indicates a heightened efficiency in scattering, which can be accredited to the interactions between the carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and the polymer matrix. These alterations in structure underscore the significance of the encapsulation process, pointing to possible applications in areas such as bio-composites, drug delivery systems, and optical materials.

The N2 adsorption and desorption isotherms observed for the CQDs-CS/CMC, as well as the DQ@CQDs-CS/CMC, conform to Type III isotherms. This classification is accompanied by hysteresis loops, which are indicative of mesoporous materials. The characterization of CQDs-CS/CMC exposed a surface area of 95.72 m2/g, a pore size of 6.57 nm, and a pore volume of 0.313 cc/g. After DQ molecules have been adsorbed, these metrics exhibited a pronounced decline, with values dropping to 68.44 m2/g for surface area, 4.2 nm for pore size, and 0.162 cc/g for pore volume. This notable reduction suggests that the presence of DQ molecules interfered with the hydrogel's structural properties by partially occupying mesopores and obstructing available adsorption sites, thereby impacting its functionality. The observed decrease in pore size substantiates the phenomenon of mesopore modification or occlusion by DQ molecules. Additionally, the presence of hysteresis loops serves as evidence for the mesoporous architecture and indicates alterations within the pore framework resulting from DQ adsorption. This underscores a distinct shift in both the structural and surface characteristics of the hydrogel beads (Fig. 2(b)).

The FT-IR range of CQDs-CS/CMC reveals distinct absorption peaks that are indicative of the different functional groups within the substance. Notably, the broad peak detected in the region of 3500–3200 cm−1 is related with the stretching vibration of OH besides NH groups. This finding suggests the attendance of hydroxyl and amino practical groups, which include characteristic components of CS/CMC hydrogels. The prominent peak observed at approximately 2920 cm−1 is indicative of CH stretching vibrations, which signify the presence of aliphatic chains within the molecular structure. The pronounced peak observed at approximately 1650 cm−1 is correlated with the stretching of the carbonyl group (CO), a characteristic indicative of amide I. Conversely, the peak situated around 1560 cm−1 corresponds to the bending of the nitrogen‑hydrogen bond (NH), signifying the presence of amide II. These findings provide confirmation of the existence of amide groups within the analyzed sample. The peak observed around 1400 cm−1 is likely a result of the CN stretching and symmetric vibrations associated with COO− groups, which are characteristic of carboxylate functionalities. The absorption band located in the range of 1100–1000 cm−1 exhibits a distinct feature associated with C–O–C stretching vibrations. This characteristic is indicative of the saccharide framework present in both CS and CMC. The attendance of these practical groups indicates the effective incorporation of CQDs into CS/CMC. This integration underscores the material's capability for adsorption and various functional uses (Fig. 2(c)).

Fig. 3(a) presents an analysis using a Scanning Electron Microscope (SEM) on Carbon Quantum Dots (CQDs), enhanced by elemental mapping through Energy-Dispersive X-ray Spectroscopy (EDX). The SEM image highlights the spherical shape and intricate structure of the CQDs. Surrounding this, elemental maps show the supply of Carbon (C), Nitrogen (N), and Oxygen (O). The Carbon map indicates high intensity, confirming it as the main element in the CQDs. The Nitrogen map, in green, shows moderate Nitrogen concentration due to surface modifications. The Oxygen map, in orange, reveals oxygen-containing groups on the CQD surface, suggesting potential functionalization. Each elemental map includes a color legend for intensity scales, and the 1 mm scale bars reference analyzed areas. This analysis details the carbon-based composition of the CQDs, with significant Nitrogen and Oxygen, indicating opportunities for surface modifications for applications like sensing and imaging.

The provided scanning electron microscope (SEM) image presents a full analysis of the assembly and elemental makeup of CQDs-CS/CMC. The primary scanning electron microscopy (SEM) image discloses a textured and porous surface morphology typical of the polymer matrix, indicating an extensive surface area that enhances the encapsulation process. In addition, the elemental mapping surrounding the micrograph illustrates the spatial distribution of oxygen (O), nitrogen (N), and carbon (C) within the sample. The carbon mapping (indicated in blue) illustrates a distribution that is relatively sparse but uniform, predominantly arising from the CQDs and the associated organic matrix. In contrast, the nitrogen mapping (represented in yellow) reveals a moderate concentration, which can be credited to the amino groups present in chitosan, thereby affirming the integration of CS. Meanwhile, the oxygen mapping (illustrated in orange) is predominant, signifying a substantial amount of carboxymethyl cellulose along with its oxygen-rich functional groups (Fig. 3(b)).

The illustration presents an EDX analysis of CQDs, featuring both an EDX spectrum and a pie chart illustrating elemental composition. The spectrum shows key peaks for Carbon (C) at ∼0.2 keV, Nitrogen (N) near 0.4 keV, and Oxygen (O) around 0.5 keV, indicating that Carbon is the primary element in CQDs, consistent with characteristics of carbon-based quantum dots. Nitrogen and Oxygen are also identified, with Nitrogen likely arising from surface modifications and Oxygen linked to oxygen-containing groups. The pie chart reveals the elemental make-up: Carbon at 63.6 %, Oxygen at 23.3 %, and Nitrogen at 13.1 %. This analysis reinforces that the CQDs are primarily Carbon, with significant Oxygen and minor Nitrogen, suggesting surface functionalization that could enhance their use in drug delivery, sensing, and imaging (Fig. 3(a)).

The pie chart and the corresponding EDX spectrum provide a quantitative analysis of the elemental composition, demonstrating that the sample consists of 74.8 % oxygen, 19.9 % nitrogen, and 5.23 % carbon. This data underscores the oxygen-rich characteristics of the composite attributable to CMC, while the presence of nitrogen supports the involvement of chitosan. Collectively, the structural and elemental evaluations corroborate the effective encapsulation of CQDs within the CS/CMC matrix. The even distribution of elements observed indicates a promising potential for stable and functional applications in adsorption process (Fig. 3(b)).

The X-ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) spectrum of C1s for CQDs-CS/CMC shows three notable peaks, each demonstrating numerous chemical conditions of carbon. The central peak, situated at 285.29 eV and comprising 49.75 % of the spectrum, is connected to CC or CH bonding (Fig. 4 ). This peak is revealing of sp2-hybridized carbon, which is commonly encountered in graphitic materials or organic compounds. This observation implies a considerable quantity of unfunctionalized carbon atoms or graphitic entities within the sample. The inferior peak perceived at 286.23 eV, accounting for 33.47 % of the spectrum, corresponds to CO bonds. This suggests the presence of hydroxyl (OH) or ether (COC) functional groups, underscoring the incorporation of oxygenated functional groups through the use of chitosan and carboxymethyl cellulose. These modifications enhance both hydrophilicity and the material's binding properties. The third peak, measured at 288.88 eV and establishing an account for 16.78 % of the overall spectrum, resembles to CO bonds that are indicative of carbonyl or carboxyl groups (COOH). This observation suggests significant oxidation states that are probably attributable to the presence of carboxymethyl cellulose, which introduces carboxyl functionalities that aid in enhancing both cross-linking capabilities and structural integrity. Collectively, these spectral features illustrate a material characterized by a harmonious combination of sp2 carbon and oxygenated functional groups, positioning it as highly appropriate for applications adsorption properties.

The N1s XPS spectrum for CQDs-CS/CMC reveals two prominent peaks, which suggest the attendance of various nitrogen chemical states in the sample. The most notable peak, situated at 400.4 eV and representing 80.38 % of the spectrum, is associated with amine (NH2) or amide (CONH) functional groups (Fig. 4). This indicates that the significant presence of nitrogen functional groups, derived from the chitosan element that naturally incorporates amino groups, shows a crucial role in augmenting both the biocompatibility and chemical reactivity of the hydrogel beads. The inferior peak identified at 403.33 eV, founding 19.62 % of the total, is related with protonated or oxidized nitrogen species including NH3 + or nitrogen oxides. This occurrence is likely a result of the interactions occurring during the synthesis or the surface modification processes. The presence of these nitrogen species enhances the surface characteristics by offering locations for hydrogen bonding and ionic interactions. The comprehensive analysis of the spectrum indicates that the nitrogen functionalities, predominantly derived from chitosan, are successfully integrated into the hydrogel.

The O1s XPS spectrum obtained from CQDs-CS/CMC displays three notable peaks. These peaks signify the presence of diverse chemical states of oxygen within the material, indicating a complex oxygen environment that plays a critical character in its overall properties. The principal peak detected at 532.38 eV, signifying 52.64 % of the total signal, is associated with CO bonds (Fig. 4). These bonds pertain to practical groups such as carbonyls or esters, which suggest the presence of functionalization through carboxymethyl cellulose and, potentially, oxidized carbon quantum dots. This prominent peak illustrates the crucial role that carbonyl functionalities play in the overall structure, thereby augmenting the hydrogel's reactivity and compatibility. The inferior peak identified at 532.56 eV, establishing an account for 35 % of the total intensity, is indicative of CO bond presence. These bonds are commonly related with practical groups like hydroxyl (OH) and ether (COC), which arise from the contributions of both chitosan and carboxymethyl cellulose. The attendance of these practical groups plays a crucial role in enhancing the material's hydrophilicity and its potential for cross-linking. The third peak identified at 534.02 eV accounts for 12.36 % of the spectrum and corresponds to the presence of adsorbed water or oxygen, manifested as COOH groups or other highly oxidized species [20]. This phenomenon likely results from the material's synthesis processes and its interactions at the surface level. In summary, the spectrum elucidates a balanced integration of oxygen functionalities predominantly influenced by the constituents of the hydrogel (Fig. 4).

The XPS spectrum obtained from the survey of CQDs-CS/CMC displays identifiable peaks that correspond to the predominant elements found in the composite material (Fig. 4). The C1s peak, situated approximately at 285 eV, stands out as the most significant feature in the analysis. This peak signifies the carbon framework of the carbon quantum dots and incorporates contributions from both CS and CMC. Its presence suggests the occurrence of sp2- and sp3-hybridized carbon, in addition to various functionalized carbon groups, including CO and CO, as evidenced by the high-resolution spectra. The N1s peak, located at approximately 400 eV, indicates the presence of nitrogen derived from the chitosan component. This nitrogen can exist in various forms, including amine (NH2), amide (CONH), or protonated nitrogen species. The prominence of this peak highlights the significance of nitrogen in the functionalization of hydrogel beads, thereby augmenting their reactivity and biocompatibility. The O1s peak, located approximately at 532 eV, is indicative of the presence of oxygen functional groups. These contain carboxyl (COOH), carbonyl (CO), and hydroxyl (OH) groups, which primarily originate from both carboxymethyl cellulose and the oxidation process of the carbon quantum dots. This peak not only reflects the hydrophilic characteristics of the material but also suggests its capability for engaging with external molecules via hydrogen bonding or ionic interactions. The survey data substantiates the material's elemental makeup, primarily consisting of carbon, oxygen, and nitrogen. This specific distribution of elements indicates a successful amalgamation of carbon quantum dots with the practical groups existent in chitosan and carboxymethyl cellulose.

Fig. 5(a) exemplifies the correlation among pH alteration and the initial pH of the aqueous solution containing CQDs-CS/CMC. The point of zero charge (pHpzc) for CQDs-CS/CMC was determined to be 5.48. This finding suggests that the surface charge of the CQDs-CS/CMC is essentially neutral in an electrostatic context. The hydrogel beads composed of CQDs-CS/CMC are expected to exhibit a positively charged surface once the pH of the solution is lesser than the point of zero charge (pHpzc). Conversely, when the pH exceeds 5.48, the surface charge of the CQDs-CS/CMC transitions to a negative charge. This demonstrates the capacity of cationic DQ to bind with CQDs-CS/CMC. Consequently, there is an important electrostatic attraction among the negatively charged CQDs-CS/CMC and cationic DQ, as exemplified in Fig. 5(a).

The pH of a solution significantly affects the effectiveness of adsorption by affecting the stability and electrical surface charge of the adsorbent particles as well as the degree of ionization and chemical stability of pesticide particles (Fig. 5(b)). The CQDs-CS/CMC exhibit distinct surface charge characteristics depending on pH levels. In particular, when the pH is lower than 5.48, these particles have a positive surface charge. On the other hand, the surface charge changes to a negative state when the pH rises above this threshold. To investigate this relationship, the result of pH on the adsorption capability of CQDs-CS/CMC was systematically examined, with the findings illustrated in Fig. 5(b). The experimental parameters for adsorption involved an initial concentration of 400 mg/L, a volume of 25 mL, a contact interval of 30 min, and an adsorbent mass of 0.02 g. The adsorption efficiency of DQ on CQDs-CS/CMC exhibits a rapid enhancement as the solution's pH transitions from 2 to 8, as depicted in Fig. 5(b). Nevertheless, beyond this specific pH range, there is a noticeable decrease in the adsorption capability as the pH continues to rise. This trend suggests that the adsorption of DQ particles onto the adsorbent particles is influenced primarily by the electrostatic interactions occurring between the charged subdivisions of CQDs-CS/CMC and the DQ particles themselves. The synthesized CQDs-CS/CMC exhibited a maximal equilibrium adsorption capacity for DQ of 3.92 mg/g at a solution pH of 8.

Study conducted an analysis of the effect of varying dosages of CQDs-CS/CMC on the adsorption capacity for DQ at room temperature. The original concentration of DQ was set at 400 mg/L, and the contact duration was maintained at 30 min. The amount of adsorbent used ranged from 0.02 to 0.5 g, with a total volume of DQ solution being 25 mL. The connection among the dosage of CQDs-CS/CMC and DQ's adsorption performance is illustrated in Fig. 5(c). As the dosage of CQDs-CS/CMC increased, there was a corresponding decline in the adsorption capability for DQ. The clearance efficacy of DQ demonstrated a positive correlation with the dosage of CQDs-CS/CMC administered. This phenomenon can be analytically interpreted as a consequence of the enhancement in the total surface area of the CQDs-CS/CMC, which, in turn, leads to an elevated number of active places. This increase in active places subsequently raises the probability of DQ molecules cooperating by the surface of the particles, thereby facilitating more effective clearance.

The initial concentrations of water pollutants, such as molecules and/or ions, produce a gradient of concentration that facilitates their migration through the adsorbent-sorbate interface and toward the adsorbent's solid surface. The number of dissolved DQ molecules that experience adsorption is therefore directly proportional to their initial concentrations in the major part solution. Fig. 5(d) illustrates the impact of varying primary DQ concentrations on the insignificant adsorption of DQ onto CQDs-CS/CMC. The experimental parameters include an initial concentration range extending from 45 to 560 mg/L, a total volume of 25 mL, a contact time of 90 min, and an adsorbent dosage of 0.02 g. In this scenario, when primary concentrations of DQ are low, there exists a greater number of active sites or localized accessible surface areas than the available adsorbate molecules. This imbalance leads to a reduction in the quantity of CQDs-CS/CMC that can be adsorbed. Conversely, as the proportion of available surface area or active place diminishes with increasing original DQ concentration, a rise in the primary DQ concentration results in a corresponding increase in the adsorption quantities of CQDs-CS/CMC.

Historically, studies have been conducted to enhance various parameters during the equilibrium phase. The consequence of the handling period on the adsorption of dacarbazine was evaluated by varying the adsorption time from 5 to 100 min, while maintaining other conditions within the optimal variety (Fig. 5(e)). The conditions for adsorption were established with an original concentration of 400 mg/L, a volume of 25 mL, a time range of 5 to 100 min, and an adsorbent amount of 0.02 g. Throughout the treatment process, the adsorption capacity of dacarbazine, as well as its elimination efficiency, exhibited an initial increase followed by a subsequent decline. The observed trend regarding the CQDs-CS/CMC hydrogel beads shows a consistent increase that persists until reaching saturation at 70 min. Subsequent extensions of the treatment duration do not appear to influence the adsorption characteristics being studied. Notably, dacarbazine exhibits the highest adsorption capacity, measured at 448 mg/g (Fig. 5(e)).

Between 20 and 45 °C, the effect of temperature on elimination efficiencies was examined. As the temperature increased, it was found that the removal efficiency of DQ improved (Fig. 5(f)). Specifically, efficiencies began to improve at temperatures exceeding 20 °C. This observation can be qualified to the instability of the hydrogen-bonded interactions among the adsorbate and adsorbent during the adsorption process, which facilitates the transition of DQ molecules from the solid stage into the main part phase.

The application of the models of adsorption isotherm, with Langmuir [21], Freundlich [22], Dubinin–Radushkevich (D-R) [23], Temkin [24], and Jossens, offers a detailed approach to analyze the adsorption characteristics of diquat (DQ) onto CQDs-CS/CMC. The Langmuir model, characterized by the presumption of monolayer adsorption on a uniform surface, is instrumental in identifying main parameters like the maximum adsorption capacity (qmax) and the equilibrium constant (KL). The Freundlich model, which is applicable to heterogeneous surfaces, provides a framework for understanding multilayer adsorption characterized by parameters like adsorption capability (KF) and intensity (n), which collectively illustrate performance across different concentration levels. Conversely, the D-R model investigates the underlying adsorption device by calculating the mean adsorption energy (E), thereby differentiating between physisorption and chemisorption processes. The Temkin model provides crucial details on the heat of adsorption and binding energy by analyzing the interactions between the adsorbent and adsorbate. This data is vital for comprehending the dynamics of these interactions. The Jossens model assesses competitive adsorption, which is particularly pertinent for intricate wastewater situations involving various contaminants. Collectively, these models offer an inclusive characterization of the adsorption procedure, yielding valuable data on surface characteristics, mechanisms, and thermodynamic principles. They aid in the refinement of operational parameters like adsorbent dosage and concentration, enhance material design, and forecast practical performance, thereby supporting scalable and sustainable approaches to wastewater treatment (Table S4).

The model of Langmuir adsorption isotherm suggestions an important empathetic of the diquat (DQ) adsorption procedure onto CQDs-CS/CMC. This analytical framework becomes particularly relevant when analyzing integral parameters, including a Langmuir constant (KL) of 0.405 L/mg, a separation factor (RL) of 0.56, and a maximum adsorption capability (qe) of 449.6 mg/g [21]. This model demonstrates that the adsorbent possess an important adsorption capability, as specified by the qe value of 449.6 mg/g. This value suggests a high efficacy in the elimination of DQ from wastewater. Furthermore, the pronounced affinity between the adsorbent and DQ molecules is supported by the KL value, underscoring the overall effectiveness of the adsorption mechanism. The separation factor (RL) of 0.56 designates a satisfactory adsorption procedure under the conditions examined, suggesting that the beads are likely to function effectively in real-world scenarios. The application of the Langmuir model, which posits monolayer adsorption on a uniform surface, facilitates the conclusion that all active sites on the hydrogel beads are exploited consistently. This facilitates the optimization of both the adsorbent dosage and the design of the system, thereby ensuring that the removal of DQ is executed in a cost-effective and efficient manner. In summary, the Langmuir model offers a comprehensive and quantitative methodology for enhancing and demonstrating the adsorption efficacy of adsorbent in the elimination of DQ from polluted water sources (Fig. 6(a)).

When illustrating the adsorption efficiency of DQ on CQDs-CS/CMC, the Freundlich adsorption isotherm model offers several advantages [22]. Notably, the high Freundlich constant (KF = 193.88 (mg/g)(L/mg)1/n) signifies a robust adsorption capability and efficiency, suggesting a favorable communication among the adsorbate and the hydrogel matrix. This model is mainly well-suited for heterogeneous systems like hydrogel beads, as it accommodates the variability in energy levels of adsorption sites, thereby mirroring conditions encountered in practical scenarios. The elevated KF value underscores the beads' proficiency in adsorbing DQ, even at minimal concentrations. Concurrently, the adsorption intensity parameter (1/n) offers analytical insights into the process's efficiency, with values ranging from 0 to 1 (0 < 1/n < 1) representative that the adsorption procedure is promising. In distinction to the Langmuir model, the Freundlich model incorporates the concept of multilayer adsorption, which is applicable in situations where several diffusion layers can develop on the surfaces of the beads. Furthermore, it facilitates a deeper comprehension of surface heterogeneity and the optimization of adsorbent dosages to enhance removal efficacy. By offering comprehensive and scalable insights, the Freundlich model serves as a significant instrument for analyzing and fine-tuning the adsorption characteristics of CQDs-CS/CMC, thereby refining the efficiency of DQ removal in wastewater action processes (Table S5).

For illustrating the adsorption properties of DQ on CQDs-CS/CMC, the Dubinin–Radushkevich (D-R) adsorption isotherm model offers particular advantages. Notably, it incorporates key parameters, including QDR valued at 415.73 mg/g and KDR at 6.015 × 10−7 mol2 kJ−2K, and would enable a thorough comprehension of the adsorption procedure. This model demonstrates to be highly effective for analyzing adsorption processes, emphasizing the energy dynamics at play while differentiating among chemical and physical adsorption mechanisms. The notable QDR value of 415.73 mg/g specifies a significant adsorption capability of the hydrogel beads, underscoring their efficiency in the process of DQ removal [23]. The KDR constant serves as an indicator of the mean free energy associated with the adsorption process, offering a framework to evaluate the nature of adsorption whether it is primarily physical (when E < 8 kJ/mol) or chemical (when E > 8 kJ/mol). In this scenario, the relatively low value of KDR implies a diminished energy of adsorption, signifying that the adsorption device is largely physical in nature. This characteristic is beneficial for the renewal and recycle of materials. The D-R model aids as an effective tool for explaining adsorption phenomena in microporous frameworks, which makes it especially relevant for the analysis of CQDs-CS/CMC likely characterized by microporosity [24]. Additionally, the D-R model facilitates an assessment of the system's performance across different environmental scenarios, thereby contributing to the optimization of operational variables pertinent to actual wastewater treatment processes. In summary, the D-R model functions as a comprehensive instrument for analyzing the adsorption mechanism and enhancing the formulation and utilization of CQDs-CS/CMC, aimed at effective DQ elimination (Fig. 6(a)).

The model of Temkin adsorption isotherm presents specific benefits in effectively characterizing the adsorption process of DQ onto CQDs-CS/CMC. Notably, it incorporates parameters like bT = 39.1 J/mol and KT = 11.96 L/mol, which contribute to a comprehensive understanding of the interaction dynamics involved. This model is distinctly appropriate for analyzing the dynamics of connections among the adsorbate and the adsorbent, as it takes into account the influence of these interactions along with the observation that the heat of adsorption reduces in a linear manner as surface coverage rises. The parameter bT, indicative of the Temkin constant associated with adsorption heat, offers valuable visions into the characteristics of the adsorption procedure. A bT value of 39.1 signifies a moderate level of heat associated with adsorption, indicating a cohesive interplay between physical and chemical adsorption processes. This balance is beneficial as it facilitates not only efficient adsorption but also the straightforward regeneration of the hydrogel beads. The equilibrium binding constant, denoted as KT = 11.96 L/mol, indicates a pronounced binding affinity between the beads and DQ molecules, thereby underscoring their effectiveness in the elimination of DQ from aqueous solutions. This model illustrates the significant role these interactions play in shaping both the adsorption capacity and overall efficiency. The model offers a nuanced perspective on the adsorption procedure by analyzing the energy distribution on the adsorbent surface. This approach becomes particularly valuable in systems characterized by fluctuating interaction energies. Furthermore, it enhances the ability to forecast adsorption efficiency across different conditions and aids in fine-tuning operational factors, including the quantity of adsorbent used and the period of contact.

The Jossens adsorption isotherm model offerings distinct benefits for depicting the adsorption procedure of DQ onto CQDs-CS/CMC, especially when specific parameters, namely K = 180.14 and J = 0.4029, are established. This model shows to be particularly useful for elucidating adsorption processes in systems characterized by intricate, heterogeneous surfaces and competitive conditions. In such scenarios, conservative models like Langmuir or Freundlich may be insufficient to accurately represent the observed behavior. The K value serves as an indicator of the adsorption capacity, elucidating the significant capability of hydrogel beads to adsorb DQ molecules, thereby underscoring their effectiveness in the remediation of contaminated wastewater. Meanwhile, the J parameter, which denotes the heterogeneity index, emphasizes the extent of surface diversity present in the adsorbent. A J value of 0.4029 indicates a surface that exhibits moderate heterogeneity. This observation is consistent with the intricate architecture of CQDs-CS/CMC, which possess diverse active sites available for adsorption. A key benefit of the Jossens model lies in its relevance to practical systems characterized by numerous interactions and competitive adsorption conditions. This feature renders it especially advantageous for wastewater treatment applications, where dissolved organic substances frequently coexist with various other pollutants. The capability of the model to incorporate surface heterogeneity and the effects of competitive adsorption donates to a more exact and thorough understanding of the adsorption procedure in these particular circumstances. In essence, the Jossens isotherm model significantly improves the analysis of adsorption processes involving CQDs-CS/CMC. It offers comprehensive insights into key factors such as adsorption capability, heterogeneity, and the dynamics of competitive adsorption (Fig. 6(a)).

The application of kinetic models, with Pseudo-First-Order [25], Pseudo-Second-Order [26], Intraparticle Diffusion [27], and Elovich [28], suggestions a thorough insight into the adsorption mechanism of DQ onto CQDs-CS/CMC. The availability of vacant adsorption sites is closely correlated with the rate of adsorption, according to the pseudo-first-order model. Because of this feature, it is especially well-suited to depicting the physical adsorption processes that take place during the early steps of the procedure, when external mass transfer has a significant impact. The text presents essential parameters such as the rate constant (K1) and equilibrium adsorption capability (qe), which are crucial for understanding adsorption dynamics. In comparison, the Pseudo-Second-Order model is more appropriate for chemisorption phenomena, as it characterizes adsorption kinetics by relating them to the square of available sites. This model provides precise estimations of both the equilibrium adsorption capacity (qe) and the adsorption rate constant (K2), rendering it particularly suitable for scenarios where chemical interactions or electron sharing occur. The Intraparticle Diffusion model examines the significance of pore diffusion as a potential bottleneck in the adsorption process. It provides a detailed analysis of the adsorption progression, which occurs in several stages: boundary layer diffusion, surface adsorption, and intraparticle diffusion. This model also assesses the extent to which internal diffusion influences the overall adsorption rates, determining its criticality in the overall mechanism. The Elovich model demonstrates significant efficacy for heterogeneous surfaces by articulating systems wherein the rates of adsorption exhibit an exponential decline over time. Critical parameters such as the initial adsorption rate (α) and the desorption constant (β) are obtained from this model. When taken as a whole, these models provide a thorough mechanistic understanding of adsorption dynamics, making it possible to pinpoint rate-limiting elements, optimize adsorbent design and operation, and forecast adsorption effectiveness in a range of scenarios. By providing critical insights into surface characteristics, interaction dynamics, and overall system performance, these models enhance the efficiency and effectiveness of DQ removal using CQDs-CS/CMC (Table S4).

Employing the Pseudo-First-Order kinetic model to represent the adsorption of DQ onto CQDs-CS/CMC provides numerous benefits, especially given that the rate constant K1 = 482.24 (min−1) × 10−2 is established. This model demonstrates significant efficacy in characterizing adsorption processes primarily governed by physical interactions, including electrostatic attractions and Van der Waals forces [25]. These connections are particularly prevalent during the initial phases of adsorption. The elevated K1 value suggests a swift rate of adsorption, highlighting the effectiveness of the CQDs-CS/CMC in rapidly sequestering DQ from wastewater [27]. The rapid onset of adsorption plays a pivotal role in scenarios where timely interventions are essential. Furthermore, the utilization of the Pseudo-First-Order model facilitates an uncomplicated determination of the equilibrium adsorption capacity (qe), thereby offering a preliminary assessment of the system's efficiency. The model facilitates an examination of how various factors, including initial DQ concentration, contact duration, and the quantity of hydrogel beads, affect the rate of adsorption (Table S6). It contributes to a mechanistic comprehension of the role that available active places on the adsorbent play in influencing the adsorption process. In summary, the Pseudo-First-Order model aids as a straightforward yet effective instrument for analyzing the kinetics of DQ adsorption on CQDs-CS/CMC. This emphasizes the rapid performance of the system and supports the optimization and scaling of adsorption operations (Fig. 6(b)).

The model of Pseudo-Second-Order kinetic presents specific benefits for depicting the adsorption process of DQ onto CQDs-CS/CMC. Notably, it incorporates key parameters, including a rate constant of K2 = 3.66 × 10−5 (g.mg−1 min−1) × 10−2 and a maximum adsorption capacity (qe) of 451.2 mg/g. These statistical figures reveal the model's efficiency in representing the dynamics of the adsorption phenomenon [26]. The low K2 value indicates a regulated rate of adsorption, implying that the process is gradual and driven by energy, suitable for systems that necessitate continuous adsorption over extended periods. This model offers accurate predictions regarding adsorption equilibrium and kinetics, facilitating insights into the interaction dynamics between DQ molecules and hydrogel beads. The optimization of critical operational limits, counting contact time, initial concentration of dissolved contaminants, and adsorbent amount is essential for achieving maximal adsorption efficiency. Additionally, the Pseudo-Second-Order model is advantageous for the scaling of wastewater treatment processes because it accurately reflects adsorption dynamics under realistic operational scenarios. The Pseudo-Second-Order model aids as a reliable framework for analyzing and enhancing the adsorption of DQ onto CQDs-CS/CMC. It emphasizes the chemisorptive characteristics of the adsorption device, substantiates the significant adsorption capacity, and offers critical information for the growth of effective, large-scale, and environmentally sustainable wastewater action solutions.

Important analytical insights into the adsorption performance of DQ on CQDs-CS/CMC are provided by the Intraparticle Diffusion model. This model is characterized by key parameters, notably Ki = 48.53 mg/g/min1/2 and X = 18.62 mg/g, which underscore the dynamics of this interaction. This model aids in evaluating whether intraparticle diffusion constitutes the rate-limiting factor in the procedure under consideration. A significant Ki value suggests that DQ molecules diffuse swiftly into the pores of the hydrogel beads, facilitating effective internal transport. The intercept X serves as an indicator of the boundary layer's impact, illustrating the significance of external diffusion during the early phases of adsorption. An examination of these parameters allows the model to provide valuable visions into the pore structure, surface area, and adsorption dynamics of the beads, thereby informing the optimization process aimed at improving their performance. The analysis delineates specific stages of adsorption, including external boundary diffusion, surface adsorption, and intraparticle transport. This differentiation is essential for comprehensively grasping and effectively managing the adsorption process. Moreover, the model demonstrates significant utility in practical wastewater treatment systems by facilitating the identification of potential bottlenecks and enhancing operational efficiency. The model of Intraparticle Diffusion offers a comprehensive analysis of the mechanisms underlying diffusion, which aids in the expansion of scalable and effective adsorption schemes. This model plays a critical role in facilitating the successful extraction of DQ through the use of CQDs-CS/CMC [27].

The Elovich adsorption isotherm model provides numerous benefits for describing the interaction of DQ with CQDs-CS/CMC. This is especially evident through its specific parameters, which include β = 142.51 g/mg and α = 0.0017 mg/g/min. The Elovich model demonstrates significant efficiency in environments characterized by heterogeneous surfaces, where the adsorption sites exhibit differing activation energies [28]. The parameter β, which corresponds to the desorption constant, illustrates that the rate of adsorption reduces as time elapses. This observation can be credited to the escalating challenge of occupying high-energy sites as the adsorption process advances. The observed β value of 142.51 indicates a significant diversity in adsorption sites present within the hydrogel beads. As the adsorption surface approaches saturation, there is a notable deceleration in the rate of adsorption. Furthermore, the α parameter, which quantifies the initial rate of adsorption, reflects a moderate value of 0.0017 mg/g/min. This suggests that the adsorption procedure occurs gradually, which can be credited to the heterogeneity of the surface and the presence of varying binding energies. The model proves to be especially advantageous for analyzing systems characterized by significant chemical interactions or a variety of energetically active sites that influence the adsorption mechanism. Additionally, the Elovich model functions to enhance operational parameters by elucidating the energy dynamics associated with adsorption processes. This understanding facilitates the effective design of adsorbents characterized by superior surface properties. This model is instrumental in enhancing the efficiency of DQ adsorption onto CQDs-CS/CMC, thereby facilitating effective wastewater treatment processes and supporting their scalability in practical applications.

Among the contributing factors that influence the adsorption process are bulk diffusion, intra-particle diffusion, external diffusion, and surface adsorption. The effects of bulk diffusion can be considerably lessened by shaking the material aggressively. Additionally, this agitation promotes quick surface adsorption. The intra-particle diffusion model aids as the primary framework for investigating the adsorption of contaminants onto adsorbents. Consequently, the research employed this specific model. Per the principles of this framework, a linear qt versus t0.5 graph that intersects at the source designates that the adsorption device is exclusively governed by intra-particle diffusion. Fig. 6(c) depicts the mechanism of intraparticle diffusion. The process of DQ absorption onto an adsorbent comprises multiple stages: initial surface adsorption, followed by particle diffusion (intercalation), and ultimately reaching a state of saturation. Using various phases shown in the intraparticle diffusion plot, the adsorption of DQ on CQDs-CS/CMC was examined at various time intervals. The preliminary linear phase demonstrates the diffusion adsorption of DQ onto the exterior surface of the CQDs-CS/CMC, which is facilitated by the surrounding solution. The internal movement of DQ, which is enabled by intramolecular mass transfer and takes place inside the CQDs-CS/CMC pores, is what defines the following phase. The final section looks at the equilibrium adsorption phase, in which the adsorbent reaches an adsorption equilibrium state due to improved internal coordination, as illustrated in Fig. 6(d).

In order to enhance the understanding of the sorption procedure, a series of tests were performed at diverse temperatures to analyze how ambient temperature influences the sorption behavior of the DQ on the sorbent. The equilibrium sorption capability of CQDs-CS/CMC demonstrated a notable increase from 318.75 to 493.75 mg/g as the temperature was raised from 293.0 to 318.0 K. This trend suggests that the affinity of the CQDs-CS/CMC for DQ is significantly enhanced at elevated temperatures. This observation supports the endothermic nature of the DQ sorption process when utilizing CQDs-CS/CMC as the sorbent material. It is evident that the enhanced affinity of DQ particles for the sorbent with increasing temperature is largely due to the increased activity of binding sites on the sorbent's surface as well as the observed decrease in the thickness of the outermost boundary layer surrounding the CQDs-CS/CMC sorbent. This probable enhanced the movement and distribution of DQ particles throughout the sorbent's pores, enabling their interaction with the accessible free binding sites (Table S4). The capacity of thermodynamic analyses to clarify the sorption mechanisms of DQ molecules onto the CQDs-CS/CMC as a sorbent is significant, as illustrated in Fig. 7(a).

The thermodynamic parameters associated with the sorption of DQ on the CQDs-CS/CMC hydrogel beads sorbent were derived through a plot of ln Kc contrary to 1/T, with the outcomes detailed in Table S7. The Gibbs free energy change (ΔG°) demonstrated a marked reduction as the temperature raised from 293 to 318 K. This trend suggests that the DQ sorption process onto the CQDs-CS/CMC becomes more spontaneous at higher temperatures (Fig. 7(b)). The positive value of ΔH° suggests that the sorption of DQ onto CQDs-CS/CMC operates as an endothermic process. The magnitude of ΔH° provides a comprehensive understanding of the interaction dynamics occurring at the solid-liquid interface among the sorbent and the sorbate (Fig. 7(c)). This reaction is classified as chemisorption due to its activation energy, which is measured at 42.6 kJ.mol−1. Additionally, the positive value of ΔS° at 349.18 J.mol−1.K−1 indicates an increase in the entropy, or randomness, within the sorption system as the DQ sorption onto the CQDs-CS/CMC sorbent approached equilibrium. The analysis also revealed that the minimum temperature for the adsorption process was 294.4 K.

The results from the adsorption studies allow for the formulation of hypotheses regarding the mechanisms fundamental the adsorption of DQ onto CQDs-CS/CMC, as demonstrated in Fig. 8 . The data indicate that in slightly alkaline conditions, the adsorption of DQ is primarily influenced by electrostatic attraction. This observation implies that electrostatic forces constitute the predominant mechanism facilitating this adsorption process. The protonated COO− groups present in the carboxymethyl cellulose and chitosan components are crucial for enhancing the adsorption of N+ ions, as they significantly contribute to the electrostatic attraction mechanism. The overall conformation of DQ molecules is meaningfully influenced by the pH levels present in the solution. Under mildly basic conditions characterized by a pH of 8.0, the dominant composition of the DQ is primarily made up of positively charged N+ ions. These ions exhibit a preferential interaction with the negatively charged regions present on the CQDs-CS/CMC, signifying an important electrostatic attraction between the two components. Improving the efficacy and efficiency of adsorbent materials requires an analysis of the role that electrostatic attraction shows in the adsorption process. An important factor in promoting the adsorption of DQ is the π-π interaction. Hydrogen bonding significantly contributes to the aptitude of CQDs-CS/CMC to adsorb DQ. This mechanism involves the creation of hydrogen bonds among the nitrogen and oxygen atoms present in DQ particles and the hydroxyl groups (OH) of the CQDs-CS/CMC. As a result, this interaction critically influences the adsorption capabilities of the adsorbent (Fig. 8). The phenomenon of pore filling transpires subsequent to the adsorption process, leading to a decrease in both pore volume besides surface area, as illustrated in Fig. 2(b).

Fig. 9 demonstrates the findings from a study examining the influence of various dissolved coexisting salts specifically Na2SO4, CaCl2, K2SO4, and MgSO4, and on the adsorption efficacy of CQDs-CS/CMC hydrogel beads concerning DQ. The coexistence of various ions like Na+, Ca2+, K+, and Mg2+ significantly influences the adsorption device of DQ onto CQDs-CS/CMC. The observed percentage reductions in DQ uptake were recorded at 86.2, 71.4, 72.6, and 84.6 %, representative a strong association between ion presence and the efficacy of DQ adsorption. The behavior of sodium and potassium ions, which exhibit a tendency to service solubilized states in aqueous environments rather than adsorbing on the surfaces of CQDs-CS/CMC hydrogel beads, suggests a possible reaction with the carboxylic groups in the DQ particles. This interaction could lead to the formation of their respective salts. Conversely, Mg2+ and Ca2+ ions have the potential to interact with the functional groups present on the active surface, which may obstruct some of the available sites. This interaction could directly contribute to a reduction in the rates of DQ removal. The variations observed in the adsorption behavior of CQDs-CS/CMC regarding DQ can be qualified to the important differences in ionic charges besides ion radii, along with the specific characteristics of the practical groups that are elaborate in the adsorption procedure. These factors, in conjunction with the attendance of different dissolved coexisting salts, contribute to the altered adsorption dynamics of the hydrogel beads (Fig. 9).

The procedure equilibrium methodology was working to assess and quantify the effectiveness of the synthesized CQDs-CS/CMC in the removal of DQ from numerous real water samples, counting drinking tap water, industrial waste, besides seawater. In this investigation, a specific mass of 0.02 g of CQDs-CS/CMC was designated for the purpose of eliminating 25 mL of DQ solution. This mass was then added to the sample, along with a 50.0 mg/L DQ concentration. DQ was extracted from wastewater, tap water, and sew water samples using a 30.0-minute shaking interval. The results from the third extraction round demonstrated recovery rates of 87.2, 96.8, and 64.7 % for industrial wastewater, tap water, and seawater, respectively. These findings suggest an effective extraction methodology, albeit with varying efficiency depending on the type of water source. Significantly, seawater showed a markedly lower retrieval rate in comparison to both tap water and wastewater.

Desorption experiments utilizing a 0.1 M of NaOH solution were conducted over a duration of 120 min at room temperature to assess the potential for reusing the CQDs-CS/CMC hydrogel beads in the decontamination of DQ from simulated wastewater. Subsequently, adjust the solution's pH to a neutral level of 7 by utilizing either 0.01 M HCl or NaOH. The ability of adsorbents to regenerate plays a critical role in enhancing their capacity for pollutant removal. The CQDs-CS/CMC hydrogel beads employed for the adsorption of pesticides, following a desorption regeneration process, demonstrated a robust removal efficiency ranging from 94 % to 97 %. Analysis of the data indicates that the pesticide removal efficiency during the initial cycle is comparable to the results achieved with freshly produced CQDs-CS/CMC hydrogel beads. Following this, the CQDs-CS/CMC hydrogel beads demonstrated a reduction in elimination effectiveness across three additional cycles, ultimately achieving a removal efficiency of 84.6 %. However, after undergoing six renewal cycles, a decline in the CQDs-CS/CMC hydrogel beads' efficacy for DQ removal was observed, as illustrated in Fig. 10(a).

After several reuse cycles, the stability of the adsorbent can be verified by X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of patterns. By comparing the XRD patterns before and after the reusability test, it is possible to determine if the CQDs-CS/CMC hydrogel beads adsorbent retains its original crystalline structure. Whereas any discernible shifts may designate changes in the crystal structure, which could indicate instability, the regularity of peak placements in the XRD pattern specifies the stability of the material. XRD measurements at various reusability stages offer a visual depiction of structural integrity and crucial details regarding the effectiveness of the adsorbent for practical uses in a variety of adsorption-desorption series (Fig. 10(b)).

Table S8 provides a comparative analysis of the maximum sorption capabilities of the CQDs-CS/CMC sorbent against numerous values sourced from current literature, along with the documented optimal working situations. The comparative analysis of sorption performance is complicated by differing experimental conditions, yet this serves as a valuable benchmark for assessing the overall potential of these materials. It is important to highlight that the CQDs-CS/CMC hydrogel beads exhibit a notable advantage attributable to their rapid sorption kinetics. The CQDs-CS/CMC's notable sorption capacity regarding DQ suggests that this sorbent may be useful in the elimination of DQ pollutants from wastewater.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Modeling experimental designs and analyzing response surfaces

ce:section.ce:para:
BBD was employed to refine the input variables associated with adsorption, specifically the solution pH designated as A, contact time labeled as B, and the dose of adsorbent referred to as C. This methodology aimed to identify the optimal output, represented by the maximum capacity of adsorption of DQ (measured in mg/g), as documented in Table 1 [29,30]. The investigation exposed that the adsorption capacity for DQ varied significantly, with values ranging from 36.4 mg/g to 448 mg/g across various experimental settings [31,32]. Within the framework of the study, several models were evaluated to understand this phenomenon, including cubic, sequential, sum of squares, linear, and quadratic models, as detailed in Table 2 . In terms of DQ elimination, the polynomial quadratic model equation's (Eq. (4)) capacity to accurately represent and elucidate the significant connections between independent input factors and the expected output replies [33,34].

Traditionally, the higher levels of the components are represented by +1 and the lower levels by −1. By examining the coefficients associated with each element, this coded formulation provides a useful tool for evaluating the factors' relative influence. The current equation, denoted as Eq. − 308.604 ∗
Dose 2

A tool for predicting the response based on specific values of every variable is the equation, which is expressed about the actual variables. Each variable's values must be expressed in their original measurement units. This equation is not suitable for figuring out the relative importance of different elements since the coefficients have been scaled to correspond with the units of each unique factor. Furthermore, the intercept fixes do not line up with the design space's center. Y stands for the response as evaluated by DQ removal, C for the adsorbent dose, B for duration, and A for pH in this context. The outcomes of the Analysis of Variance (ANOVA) for the specified model are presented in Table 3 . The model, derived from the Box-Behnken Design (BBD), assessing the adsorption capability of DQ over time demonstrated a high level of significance. The p-value, which is <0.05, suggests that the model is a valid representation of the data. Notably, all terms, with the exception of the squared adsorbent dose component, showed statistical significance. In this analysis, the F-value of the model was determined to be 93.16, with a corresponding p-value of <0.0001. The influence of the adsorbent dose was notably pronounced, evidenced by its F-value of 58.36. This suggests that the removal of DQ is significantly influenced by properties such as the obtainability of active adsorption places, as well as the surface functionality and porosity of the adsorbent. The effect of interaction time significantly affects the adsorption and removal of DQ, as evidenced by a high F-value of 649.76. This finding suggests that contact time is an important factor in the adsorption and elimination of DQ, highlighting its vital role in the overall adsorption process. The regression coefficient (R2) was calculated to be 0.9917, indicating that a mere 0.0083 % of the variance remains unexplained by the model, attributed to error. Furthermore, the predicted R2 and the adjusted R2 values for the model were originated to be 0.8675 and 0.9811, individually. The elevated R2 values demonstrate a strong concordance and contribute positively to the model's validity. The absence of a significant ft test can be attributed to pure error, indicating that if this term registers as non-significant, the model's accuracy is upheld. Furthermore, the signal-to-noise ratio measures at 33.461, exceeding the threshold of 4. This provides robust justification for its applicability in optimizing space design, specifically in predicting responses related to DQ adsorption capacity. In Fig. 11(a), the real and predicted data produced by the model exhibit a linear distribution, with a minimal number of points falling outside this linear trend. Similarly, a usual probability plot in Fig. 11(b) against the highly standardized residuals reveals comparable results, reflected in the respective percentage of DQ. Consequently, the association between the experiment and the model's output response is notable. Furthermore, the correlation between the residuals and the DQ adsorption process is illustrated in Fig. 11(c, d). A strong and effective model is shown by the wide distribution of data points close to the zero axis and their notable aggregation in this area. It is possible to find any missed or surprising values or trends that the model might not have picked up by examining the differences between expected response values and actual data. An efficient technique for determining the best power transformation purpose for the reply variable is to use the Box-Cox curves shown in Fig. 11(e). Specifically, the Box-Cox plot's minimum point indicates the optimal Lambda value, or the shortest residual sum of squares for the revised model. Because it yields the lowest residual sum of squares in the revised model, this lowest point on the Box-Cox graph is the optimal Lambda value. High performance is shown by the graphs demonstrating the model's suitability. One variable was changed while all other variables remained unchanged in order to evaluate the yield response. It is clear that every variable has a beneficial effect on the adsorption capacity; nevertheless, as the figure illustrates, the duration, pH, and dosage are the most important factors. The correlation between pH levels and the duration of the adsorption process showed how quickly these factors affected the adsorption capacity. Throughout the adsorption process, the variables of duration, dose, and pH all demonstrated a positive link with the adsorption capacity (Fig. 11(f)).

To gain a comprehensive understanding of how these variables impact the elimination of DQ, three-dimensional surface response plots were utilized to examine the main correlations between the components being studied. Fig. 12(a) demonstrates the influence of variations in pH and the dosage of CQDs-CS/CMC on the adsorption capacity for DQ, with each experimental alteration being conducted over a duration of 100 min. An increase in pH from 2 to 8 correlated with an enhanced capacity for DQ adsorption, as exemplified in Fig. 12(b). The most advantageous conditions were found to be a pH of 8 in conjunction with an adsorbent dosage of 0.02 g for a 25 mL volume. In contrast, the capacity for adsorption increased at a pH of 8. The designation of pH 8 as the most effective parameter stems from the attendance of a positive charge on DQ and the pHpzc characteristic of CQDs-CS/CMC, which is recorded at 5.48. Consequently, the adsorption process was determined to be enhanced when the pH is maintained above 5.48. Fig. 12(b) demonstrates how modifications in the dosage and timing of CQDs-CS/CMC influence the adsorption capacity of DQ. Furthermore, the data indicates that the capacity to absorb increased with contact duration, reaching its maximum at 0.2 g of adsorbent and a hundred minutes of extended contact. Fig. 12(c) exemplifies the influence of different exposure durations and pH levels on the adsorption capability of CQDs-CS/CMC for DQ. The absorption capacity first rises from 2 to 8 and then falls as pH levels rise, particularly in alkaline circumstances. Furthermore, the absorption capacity was observed to enhance with prolonged contact time.

The ideal predictable values for the investigational conditions required to gain the maximum response in terms of adsorption capability are shown in Fig. 13(a). The results of the desirability purpose are shown in Fig. 13(b). Each variable's reaction was evaluated in this study within predetermined parameters. In particular, the temperature was kept between 20 and 45 °C, the concentration C0 ranged from 45 to 560 mg/L, and the contact time was between 5 and 100 min. Strong agreement was found between the experiment's results and the expected reactions. This demonstrated that the model was suitable and sufficient. Refer to Fig. 13(c). Following this, two agreement experiments were showed utilizing the optimized input parameters. The experiment's results showed how accurate and effective the BBD is when used in conjunction with the intended goal of determining the optimal adsorption limits to achieve maximum output. As a result, additional research on thermodynamics, kinetics, and isotherms was carried out within these ideal parameters.

The investigation presents a novel formulation of CQDs-CS/CMC hydrogel beads, achieved by embedding carbon quantum dots (CQDs) into the composite matrix composed of carboxymethyl cellulose (CMC) and chitosan, two naturally derived polymers. This integration process is enabled by a connection with epichlorohydrin, yielding a product that exhibits both ecological sustainability and compatibility. An analytical assessment of the CQDs-CS/CMC components was conducted employing various methodologies, which comprised nitrogen adsorption/desorption isotherm analysis, FT-IR, SEM with EDX, and XRD techniques. The results revealed that the CQDs-CS/CMC exhibited a specific surface area of 95.72 m2/g, a pore diameter measuring 6.57 nm, and a pore volume of 0.313 cc/g. Next the adsorption of DQ, a notable decrease in various metrics was observed, with the surface area falling to 68.44 m2/g, the pore size decreasing to 4.2 nm, and the pore volume reducing to 0.162 cc/g. This decline indicates that the presence of DQ molecules significantly influences the reduction in surface area and pore volume, likely due to their partial occupancy of mesopores, which leads to the obstruction of available adsorption sites. The BBD methodology was implemented to assess the adsorption capacity of CQDs-CS/CMC about DQ. This analysis aimed to elucidate the interconnections among influences like DQ concentration, pH levels, adsorption time, and temperature fluctuations. Examining the response surface obtained from the empirical model designates an insignificant association among the length of adsorption time and temperature. To attain optimal adsorption efficiency, various critical factors necessitate further investigation. The expected peak adsorption capacity is roughly 449.6 mg/g, contingent upon particular parameters: a time frame of 100 min, a controlled temperature of 25 °C, and an initial pH value established at 8. An increase in temperature has been shown to render the adsorption process endothermic, which in turn amplifies the overall adsorption capacity achieved. Following the execution of six successive cycles of adsorption and desorption, an calculation of the adsorbent's reusability demonstrated that it preserved its structural integrity, with its capability to remove pollutants remaining relatively consistent. Furthermore, while its chemical structure remained intact, the material showcased considerable effectiveness, as corroborated by the stable XRD data collected both before and following numerous applications.
